# Complete Push Notifications & Backend Integration Guide

This guide details the complete configuration, database schemas, backend code, and manual testing procedures required to run the notifications system successfully.

---

## 1. Supabase SQL Database Migration

Run the following SQL commands in your Supabase project's **SQL Editor** to create the tables required for push tokens, scheduled reminders, and notifications history:

```sql
-- 1. Table: user_push_tokens
-- Stores Expo Push Tokens generated by users' mobile devices.
create table if not exists public.user_push_tokens (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id uuid references auth.users(id) on delete cascade not null,
  expo_push_token text not null,
  created_at timestamp with time zone default timezone('utc'::text, now()) not null,
  unique(user_id, expo_push_token)
);

-- Indexing for rapid queries during notification lookups
create index if not exists idx_user_push_tokens_user_id on public.user_push_tokens(user_id);

-- 2. Table: reminder_notifications
-- Tracks local notifications scheduled on the mobile client.
create table if not exists public.reminder_notifications (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id uuid references auth.users(id) on delete cascade not null,
  event_id text not null,
  notification_id text not null,
  scheduled_for timestamp with time zone not null,
  created_at timestamp with time zone default timezone('utc'::text, now()) not null
);

create index if not exists idx_reminder_notifications_user_id on public.reminder_notifications(user_id);
create index if not exists idx_reminder_notifications_event_id on public.reminder_notifications(event_id);

-- 3. Table: notifications
-- Holds notifications history visible on a profile inbox or notification history screen.
create table if not exists public.notifications (
  id uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id uuid references auth.users(id) on delete cascade not null,
  title text not null,
  body text not null,
  type text not null, -- 'reminder', 'feature', 'social', 'system', 'marketing'
  is_read boolean default false not null,
  created_at timestamp with time zone default timezone('utc'::text, now()) not null
);

create index if not exists idx_notifications_user_id on public.notifications(user_id);
create index if not exists idx_notifications_is_read on public.notifications(is_read);

-- Enable Row Level Security (RLS) for privacy
alter table public.user_push_tokens enable row level security;
alter table public.reminder_notifications enable row level security;
alter table public.notifications enable row level security;

-- Policies: Allow authenticated users to manage their own records
create policy "Allow user access to own push tokens" on public.user_push_tokens
  for all using (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "Allow user access to own reminder mappings" on public.reminder_notifications
  for all using (auth.uid() = user_id);

create policy "Allow user access to own notifications history" on public.notifications
  for all using (auth.uid() = user_id);
```

---

## 2. Supabase Edge Function Deployment

### Step 1: Initialize and Create Edge Function
On your development machine, open your terminal inside your Supabase project directory and run:
```bash
supabase functions new send-notification
```

### Step 2: Implement the Function Code
Replace the contents of `supabase/functions/send-notification/index.ts` with the following production-ready Deno code:

```typescript
import { serve } from "https://deno.land/std@0.168.0/http/server.ts";

const corsHeaders = {
  "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "*",
  "Access-Control-Allow-Headers": "authorization, x-client-info, apiKey, content-type",
};

serve(async (req) => {
  // Handle CORS preflight options request
  if (req.method === "OPTIONS") {
    return new Response("ok", { headers: corsHeaders });
  }

  try {
    const { token, title, body, data } = await req.json();

    if (!token) {
      return new Response(JSON.stringify({ error: "Missing recipient token." }), {
        status: 400,
        headers: { ...corsHeaders, "Content-Type": "application/json" },
      });
    }

    // Call the Expo Push Notification service endpoint
    const response = await fetch("https://exp.host/--/api/v2/push/send", {
      method: "POST",
      headers: {
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Accept": "application/json",
      },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        to: token,
        sound: "default",
        title: title || "Farmora Update",
        body: body || "You have a new alert.",
        data: data || {},
        priority: "high",
        channelId: "default", // Maps to Android channel name
      }),
    });

    const result = await response.json();

    return new Response(JSON.stringify({ success: true, result }), {
      status: 200,
      headers: { ...corsHeaders, "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    });
  } catch (error: any) {
    return new Response(JSON.stringify({ error: error.message }), {
      status: 500,
      headers: { ...corsHeaders, "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    });
  }
});
```

### Step 3: Deploy the Edge Function
Deploy the function live to your Supabase project dashboard:
```bash
supabase functions deploy send-notification
```

---

## 3. EAS Build & Expo Configuration

To trigger native push notifications in standalone production builds (`EAS Build`), you must specify an EAS **projectId** in your config file.

### Step 1: Locate `app.json`
Confirm your `app.json` has `plugins` and `extra.eas.projectId` configured. Under the `expo` object, add:

```json
{
  "expo": {
    "name": "Farmora",
    "slug": "farmora",
    "plugins": [
      [
        "expo-notifications",
        {
          "icon": "./assets/images/icon.png",
          "color": "#006e2f"
        }
      ]
    ],
    "extra": {
      "eas": {
        "projectId": "YOUR-EAS-PROJECT-ID-GOES-HERE"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

*Note: Replace `YOUR-EAS-PROJECT-ID-GOES-HERE` with your project's unique ID from your Expo dev console.*

---

## 4. Testing Instructions

### A. Testing Local Reminders
1. Open the app on an iOS Simulator or Android Emulator.
2. Tap the **Calendar** tab.
3. Tap **Add Task**. Fill in details for a task set **in the future** (e.g. 5 minutes from now) and hit submit.
4. You will see a success console log indicating the notification has been scheduled.
5. Delete the task, and verify that the notification cancel logic is triggered.

### B. Testing Push Notifications via Supabase Trigger
1. Log in with a real email account (not a mock user).
2. The app will automatically ask for notification permission and retrieve the push token, then upsert it to Supabase in the `user_push_tokens` table.
3. Open your Supabase Dashboard -> **SQL Editor**, and retrieve your user's push token:
   ```sql
   select expo_push_token from public.user_push_tokens limit 1;
   ```
4. Trigger a push notification by sending a POST request to your Supabase Edge Function (e.g., using Curl, Postman, or Hoppscotch):
   ```bash
   curl -i -X POST https://YOUR_PROJECT_REF.supabase.co/functions/v1/send-notification \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{
       "token": "YOUR_RETRIEVED_EXPO_PUSH_TOKEN",
       "title": "PM-KISAN scheme update!",
       "body": "Your subsidy registration deadline is approaching in 2 days.",
       "data": { "screen": "/(tabs)/calendar" }
     }'
   ```
5. Observe the notification pop up on your device. Click the notification, and notice the app navigates you instantly to the **Calendar** screen.
